result:
# The taste of rain

Rain tastes like a promise unwrapping itself. Not sweet or sour, just honest—like the first day of school felt in the mouth. The sky holds its breath, then lets go, and every surface in the neighborhood learns a new language. Pavement goes soft and dark, releasing a warm, mineral scent that travels up your sleeves. On tin roofs it rings in silver, drumming a rhythm that reminds you of things you can’t name. In parks, dust dissolves into earth, and the air smells like green and stone and a hint of lemon. Cars splash and glide, headlights smearing into slow comets. A child runs barefoot, then stops, grinning, as a puddle climbs his ankles. Umbrellas bloom, black flowers on a gray river. We become polite strangers, sharing the edge of an awning without speaking. I taste ozone and cedar and something I can only call future, because it feels like all the days ahead have opened their doors. The sidewalk turns slippery, but the mind turns kinder. Time dilates and shortens at once; the storm is here and not here. We pause, and for a moment we believe that change is as simple as water falling. It teaches us to wait, and to receive, and to let go, finally.

On tin roofs it rings in silver, drumming a rhythm that reminds you of things you can’t name. In parks, dust dissolves into earth, and the air smells like green and stone and a hint of lemon. Cars splash and glide, headlights smearing into slow comets. A child runs barefoot, then stops, grinning, as a puddle climbs his ankles. Umbrellas bloom, black flowers on a gray river. We become polite strangers, sharing the edge of an awning without speaking. Time dilates and shortens at once; the storm is here and not here. We pause, and for a moment we believe that change is as simple as water falling. It teaches us to wait, and to receive, and to let go, finally.
